summary: The q5 dataset shows uniform activity across all ten problems (q1-q10), each carrying exactly 100 issues, with no problem standing out by volume. The flat distribution and lack of per-problem detail or proof-status differentiation make it impossible to identify natural traction leaders or near-complete proofs from the provided context alone.
problems: All ten problems (q1-q10) report an identical count of 100 issues, suggesting either a capped/truncated dataset or a placeholder value rather than genuine per-problem activity signals.
The 'Issues Per Problem' section is empty, so issue distribution cannot be analyzed at the granularity needed to rank traction.
The 'Proof Status' section only lists raw issue counts and contains no actual proof state (e.g., open, in-progress, verified), preventing assessment of solvability.
No metadata on issue types, recency, or assignees is available, making it hard to distinguish active discussion from stale backlog.
With every problem equally weighted at 100, prioritization risk is high: real signal is masked by uniform counts.
highlights: A complete, consistent dataset covering all ten target problems is available, providing a stable baseline for tracking.
Uniform issue counts indicate systematic data collection rather than gaps in coverage.
The structure is ready for enrichment—once proof-status labels and timestamps are added, ranking and solvability prediction become straightforward.

suggested_todos.title: Investigate why all problems show exactly 100 issues; confirm whether this is a data cap, truncation, or genuine count
Enrich the Proof Status section with actual proof states (open/in-progress/verified) to enable solvability assessment
Add issue-level metadata (creation date, type, assignee) to surface true per-problem traction
Re-run the insight report after data enrichment to identify genuine priority problems for the week
Set up automated validation to flag suspicious uniform counts in future dataset ingests
